team_name: Institute for Systems Genetics pair: David Fenyo + Paolo Mita
opportunity_title: SPURRING PROJECTS TO ADVANCE ENERGY RESEARCH AND KNOWLEDGE SWIFTLY (SPARKS)
suggested_specific_aim: Define a focused pilot that ties the opportunity deliverable to an existing departmental strength and a measurable endpoint.
next_steps: Confirm eligibility and scope, assign a lead, draft a one page concept, and map resources and preliminary data needs.
suggested_title: DE-FOA-0003164 — ISG: tools, including, which, processes
synopsis: This application would address SPURRING PROJECTS TO ADVANCE ENERGY RESEARCH AND KNOWLEDGE SWIFTLY (SPARKS) by integrating complementary expertise across the team. The proposed collaboration is centered on tools, including, which, processes and is designed to produce a cohesive, milestone-driven set of deliverables.
rationale_detailed: Faculty were selected for strong topical overlap with the opportunity and complementary, non-redundant strengths. Key contributions: David Fenyo: In my research I apply mathematical, statistical and computational methods to provide a detailed understanding of the dynamics of cellular processes through the integration and visualization of data from multiple technologies—including mass spectrometry, next generation sequencing, and microscopy to provide a deeper understanding of biological systems. My focus is the analysis of analytical measurements: the estimation of the information content of these measurements from different experimental designs, and the optimal use of such information to yield statistically meaningful answers to biological questions.; Paolo Mita: Transposable elements (TE) are sequences of DNA that are capable of moving within a host genome, and thus are often referred to as “jumping genes.” DNA transposons use a “cut and paste” mechanism to move within a host genome while retrotransposons increase their amount using a “copy and paste” process. My research focuses on the study of the Long Interspersed Element-1 (LINE-1 or L1) retrotransposon, which is the only TE capable of autonomously "jumping" within the human genome..
suggested_aims: Aim 1: Define and quantify the core problem space in tools, including, which, processes using the team’s established models and datasets.
Aim 2: Develop and optimize an integrated approach that leverages each investigator’s strengths to address the opportunity requirements.
Aim 3: Validate outcomes in the most relevant system and deliver a clear translational or mechanistic endpoint aligned to the FOA.

team_name: Institute for Systems Genetics pair: David Fenyo + Ran Brosh

suggested_title: DE-FOA-0003467 — ISG: apply, including, have, research
synopsis: This application would address SEEDING CRITICAL ADVANCES FOR LEADING ENERGY TECHNOLOGIES WITH UNTAPPED POTENTIAL (SCALEUP) READY by integrating complementary expertise across the team. The proposed collaboration is centered on apply, including, have, research and is designed to produce a cohesive, milestone-driven set of deliverables.
rationale_detailed: Faculty were selected for strong topical overlap with the opportunity and complementary, non-redundant strengths. Key contributions: David Fenyo: In my research I apply mathematical, statistical and computational methods to provide a detailed understanding of the dynamics of cellular processes through the integration and visualization of data from multiple technologies—including mass spectrometry, next generation sequencing, and microscopy to provide a deeper understanding of biological systems. My focus is the analysis of analytical measurements: the estimation of the information content of these measurements from different experimental designs, and the optimal use of such information to yield statistically meaningful answers to biological questions.; Ran Brosh: I have two roles: [1] as Faculty at ISG/Pathology I am leading the Dark Matter Project, a collaborative effort to use synthetic biology and genome writing to study the noncoding genome and its roles in health and disease. We develop method to build and deliver large DNA constructs to mammalian cells (primarily mouse embryonic stem cells) and apply them to generate mouse models for human diseases or in vitro cell models to study basic aspects of transcriptional regulation..
suggested_aims: Aim 1: Define and quantify the core problem space in apply, including, have, research using the team’s established models and datasets.
Aim 2: Develop and optimize an integrated approach that leverages each investigator’s strengths to address the opportunity requirements.
Aim 3: Validate outcomes in the most relevant system and deliver a clear translational or mechanistic endpoint aligned to the FOA.

suggested_title: PAR-26-040 — ISG: biology, research, approaches, methods
synopsis: This application would address Advancing Bioinformatics, Translational Bioinformatics and Computational Biology Research (R01 Clinical Trial Optional) by integrating complementary expertise across the team. The proposed collaboration is centered on biology, research, approaches, methods and is designed to produce a cohesive, milestone-driven set of deliverables.
rationale_detailed: Faculty were selected for strong topical overlap with the opportunity and complementary, non-redundant strengths. Key contributions: David Fenyo: In my research I apply mathematical, statistical and computational methods to provide a detailed understanding of the dynamics of cellular processes through the integration and visualization of data from multiple technologies—including mass spectrometry, next generation sequencing, and microscopy to provide a deeper understanding of biological systems. My focus is the analysis of analytical measurements: the estimation of the information content of these measurements from different experimental designs, and the optimal use of such information to yield statistically meaningful answers to biological questions.; Ran Brosh: I have two roles: [1] as Faculty at ISG/Pathology I am leading the Dark Matter Project, a collaborative effort to use synthetic biology and genome writing to study the noncoding genome and its roles in health and disease. We develop method to build and deliver large DNA constructs to mammalian cells (primarily mouse embryonic stem cells) and apply them to generate mouse models for human diseases or in vitro cell models to study basic aspects of transcriptional regulation..

Key contributions: Paolo Mita: Transposable elements (TE) are sequences of DNA that are capable of moving within a host genome, and thus are often referred to as “jumping genes.” DNA transposons use a “cut and paste” mechanism to move within a host genome while retrotransposons increase their amount using a “copy and paste” process. My research focuses on the study of the Long Interspersed Element-1 (LINE-1 or L1) retrotransposon, which is the only TE capable of autonomously "jumping" within the human genome.; Molly Gale Hammell: Molly Gale Hammell is an Associate Professor in the Institute for Systems Genetics at NYU Langone Health. Key contributions: David Fenyo: In my research I apply mathematical, statistical and computational methods to provide a detailed understanding of the dynamics of cellular processes through the integration and visualization of data from multiple technologies—including mass spectrometry, next generation sequencing, and microscopy to provide a deeper understanding of biological systems. My focus is the analysis of analytical measurements: the estimation of the information content of these measurements from different experimental designs, and the optimal use of such information to yield statistically meaningful answers to biological questions.; Jef D. Boeke: Leads a synthetic genomics and genome writing program focused on designing, assembling, and installing very large DNA constructs to interrogate genome regulation and build faithful disease models. Develops genome writing pipelines that combine yeast based assembly of multi segment loci with targeted delivery into yeast and mouse embryonic stem cells using strategies such as Big IN and mSwAP In..

next_steps: Confirm eligibility and scope, assign a lead, draft a one page concept, and map resources and preliminary data needs.
suggested_title: PAR-25-244 — ISG: clinical, tools, research, genetic
synopsis: This application would address Basic Research in Cancer Health Disparities (R21 Clinical Trial Not Allowed) by integrating complementary expertise across the team. The proposed collaboration is centered on clinical, tools, research, genetic and is designed to produce a cohesive, milestone-driven set of deliverables.
rationale_detailed: Faculty were selected for strong topical overlap with the opportunity and complementary, non-redundant strengths. Key contributions: Paolo Mita: Transposable elements (TE) are sequences of DNA that are capable of moving within a host genome, and thus are often referred to as “jumping genes.” DNA transposons use a “cut and paste” mechanism to move within a host genome while retrotransposons increase their amount using a “copy and paste” process. My research focuses on the study of the Long Interspersed Element-1 (LINE-1 or L1) retrotransposon, which is the only TE capable of autonomously "jumping" within the human genome.; Molly Gale Hammell: Molly Gale Hammell is an Associate Professor in the Institute for Systems Genetics at NYU Langone Health. Her research portfolio centers on computational and quantitative genetic approaches, focusing on the regulation and function of transposable elements in the human genome, RNA biology, and data-driven studies of neurodegeneration, particularly Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS)..
suggested_aims: Aim 1: Define and quantify the core problem space in clinical, tools, research, genetic using the team’s established models and datasets.
Aim 2: Develop and optimize an integrated approach that leverages each investigator’s strengths to address the opportunity requirements.
Aim 3: Validate outcomes in the most relevant system and deliver a clear translational or mechanistic endpoint aligned to the FOA.

team_name: Institute for Systems Genetics pair: David Fenyo + Jef D. Boeke
opportunity_title: BRAIN Initiative: Theories, Models and Methods for Analysis of Complex Data from the Brain (R01 Clinical Trial Not Allowed)
suggested_specific_aim: Define a focused pilot that ties the opportunity deliverable to an existing departmental strength and a measurable endpoint.
next_steps: Confirm eligibility and scope, assign a lead, draft a one page concept, and map resources and preliminary data needs.
suggested_title: RFA-DA-27-004 — ISG: systems, such, models, including
synopsis: This application would address BRAIN Initiative: Theories, Models and Methods for Analysis of Complex Data from the Brain (R01 Clinical Trial Not Allowed) by integrating complementary expertise across the team. The proposed collaboration is centered on systems, such, models, including and is designed to produce a cohesive, milestone-driven set of deliverables.
rationale_detailed: Faculty were selected for strong topical overlap with the opportunity and complementary, non-redundant strengths. Key contributions: David Fenyo: In my research I apply mathematical, statistical and computational methods to provide a detailed understanding of the dynamics of cellular processes through the integration and visualization of data from multiple technologies—including mass spectrometry, next generation sequencing, and microscopy to provide a deeper understanding of biological systems. My focus is the analysis of analytical measurements: the estimation of the information content of these measurements from different experimental designs, and the optimal use of such information to yield statistically meaningful answers to biological questions.; Jef D. Boeke: Leads a synthetic genomics and genome writing program focused on designing, assembling, and installing very large DNA constructs to interrogate genome regulation and build faithful disease models. Develops genome writing pipelines that combine yeast based assembly of multi segment loci with targeted delivery into yeast and mouse embryonic stem cells using strategies such as Big IN and mSwAP In..
suggested_aims: Aim 1: Define and quantify the core problem space in systems, such, models, including using the team’s established models and datasets.
Aim 2: Develop and optimize an integrated approach that leverages each investigator’s strengths to address the opportunity requirements.
Aim 3: Validate outcomes in the most relevant system and deliver a clear translational or mechanistic endpoint aligned to the FOA.

suggested_title: DE-FOA-0003164 — ISG: including, research, apply, tools
synopsis: This application would address SPURRING PROJECTS TO ADVANCE ENERGY RESEARCH AND KNOWLEDGE SWIFTLY (SPARKS) by integrating complementary expertise across the team. rationale_detailed: Faculty were selected for strong topical overlap with the opportunity and complementary, non-redundant strengths. Key contributions: David Fenyo: In my research I apply mathematical, statistical and computational methods to provide a detailed understanding of the dynamics of cellular processes through the integration and visualization of data from multiple technologies—including mass spectrometry, next generation sequencing, and microscopy to provide a deeper understanding of biological systems. My focus is the analysis of analytical measurements: the estimation of the information content of these measurements from different experimental designs, and the optimal use of such information to yield statistically meaningful answers to biological questions.; Paolo Mita: Transposable elements (TE) are sequences of DNA that are capable of moving within a host genome, and thus are often referred to as “jumping genes.” DNA transposons use a “cut and paste” mechanism to move within a host genome while retrotransposons increase their amount using a “copy and paste” process. My research focuses on the study of the Long Interspersed Element-1 (LINE-1 or L1) retrotransposon, which is the only TE capable of autonomously "jumping" within the human genome.; Ran Brosh: I have two roles: [1] as Faculty at ISG/Pathology I am leading the Dark Matter Project, a collaborative effort to use synthetic biology and genome writing to study the noncoding genome and its roles in health and disease. We develop method to build and deliver large DNA constructs to mammalian cells (primarily mouse embryonic stem cells) and apply them to generate mouse models for human diseases or in vitro cell models to study basic aspects of transcriptional regulation..
